activities.title: Distinguish Positive-Sense and Negative-Sense RNA Viruses
mapped_learning_outcomes: Explain the differences between positive-sense and negative-sense RNA viruses in terms of genome translation and replication.

required_materials.purpose: Reference material on RNA virus genome types
required_materials.specification: Concise text explaining positive-sense and negative-sense RNA virus genome characteristics, translation requirements, and replication mechanisms.
Table with rows for genome type, translation mechanism, replication enzyme, and example viruses for learner completion.

activities.learner_task: Using the provided reference text, complete the comparison table by filling in the key differences between positive-sense and negative-sense RNA viruses, focusing on genome translation and replication.
activities.expected_output: A completed comparison table accurately showing distinctions in genome type, translation process, replication enzyme requirements, and examples of each virus type.
activities.activity_preamble: Understanding how RNA viruses differ in their genome translation and replication is fundamental to grasping their biology. This activity helps you clarify these differences by organizing key features side-by-side.
activities.prior_knowledge_activation: Recall basic concepts of RNA and protein synthesis from molecular biology.
activities.reasoning_orientation: Compare and contrast key mechanistic features of two virus genome types to clarify functional differences.
activities.self_explanation_prompt: Explain in your own words why positive-sense RNA can be directly translated but negative-sense RNA cannot.
activities.evidence_use_prompt: Refer to the provided text to support your table entries with specific terminology and examples.
activities.conceptual_contrast_prompt: Focus on contrasting genome polarity and its impact on translation and replication, avoiding confusion between genome type and virus family.

activities.title: Explore Hepatitis C Virus Features and Host Interaction
mapped_learning_outcomes: Describe the key features of Hepatitis C virus (HCV), including its classification, replication site, and role of host microRNA-122.

required_materials.specification: Text excerpt describing HCV as a positive-sense flavivirus, its replication in hepatocytes, and interaction with host microRNA-122.
Worksheet prompting learners to list HCV classification, replication site, and the function of microRNA-122.

activities.learner_task: Read the HCV overview extract and complete the summary worksheet by listing the virus classification, its replication site, and explaining the role of host microRNA-122 in stabilizing the viral genome.
activities.expected_output: A filled worksheet clearly stating HCV as a positive-sense flavivirus, its replication in hepatocytes, and a concise explanation of microRNA-122’s stabilizing role on the HCV 5' UTR.
activities.activity_preamble: Hepatitis C virus has unique features that influence its replication and interaction with the host. This activity guides you to identify these critical aspects and understand their biological significance.
activities.prior_knowledge_activation: Recall what you know about virus-host interactions and RNA stability mechanisms.
activities.reasoning_orientation: Identify and explain biological roles of viral and host factors in HCV replication.
activities.self_explanation_prompt: Summarize why microRNA-122 is important for HCV replication before checking your worksheet.
activities.evidence_use_prompt: Use direct phrases from the extract to support your explanations.
activities.conceptual_contrast_prompt: Distinguish HCV’s host interaction mechanisms from general virus replication strategies.

activities.title: Identify the Role of HCV NS5B Protein in Viral Replication
mapped_learning_outcomes: Identify the function of the HCV NS5B protein and its importance in viral replication.

required_materials.specification: Text explaining NS5B as the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase essential for HCV genome replication.
Set of questions prompting learners to define NS5B’s function, mechanism, and significance.

activities.learner_task: Read the description of NS5B and answer the guided questions to explain its role and importance in HCV replication.
activities.expected_output: Clear, concise answers describing NS5B as the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase that synthesizes new viral RNA genomes, highlighting its essential role in viral replication.
activities.activity_preamble: The NS5B protein is a key enzyme in HCV replication. Understanding its function helps clarify how the virus copies its RNA genome.
activities.reasoning_orientation: Focus on mechanistic explanation of enzyme function within viral replication.
activities.self_explanation_prompt: Before answering, try to explain how NS5B contributes to the viral life cycle in your own words.
activities.evidence_use_prompt: Support your answers with specific terms from the NS5B description.
activities.conceptual_contrast_prompt: Avoid confusing NS5B with host polymerases or other viral proteins.

activities.title: Clarify Misconceptions: HCV vs Influenza and Retroviruses
mapped_learning_outcomes: Differentiate between RNA virus genome segmentation and integration mechanisms, specifically clarifying common misconceptions about HCV compared to influenza and retroviruses.

required_materials.specification: Text explaining influenza genome segmentation, retrovirus integration, and clarifying that HCV does not integrate into host DNA nor have segmented genome.
Table with columns for virus, genome segmentation, integration ability, and common misconceptions for learner completion.

activities.learner_task: Using the misconceptions briefing, complete the correction table by identifying which viruses have segmented genomes or integrate into host DNA, and explain why HCV differs from influenza and retroviruses.
activities.expected_output: A completed table correctly showing influenza as segmented, retroviruses as integrating, and HCV as non-segmented and non-integrating, with brief explanations correcting common misconceptions.
activities.activity_preamble: Common misconceptions about viral genome organization and replication can hinder understanding. This activity helps you distinguish HCV’s biology from influenza and retroviruses by correcting typical errors.
activities.reasoning_orientation: Analyze and contrast virus genome structures and replication strategies to repair misconceptions.
activities.self_explanation_prompt: Before completing the table, write a sentence explaining why HCV is often confused with these other viruses.
activities.evidence_use_prompt: Quote or paraphrase key points from the briefing to support your corrections.
activities.conceptual_contrast_prompt: Focus on contrasting segmentation and integration mechanisms, avoiding conflation of these distinct viral features.

A1: Explain the differences between positive-sense and negative-sense RNA viruses in terms of genome translation and replication.
A2: Describe the key features of Hepatitis C virus (HCV), including its classification, replication site, and role of host microRNA-122.
A3: Identify the function of the HCV NS5B protein and its importance in viral replication.
A4: Differentiate between RNA virus genome segmentation and integration mechanisms, specifically clarifying common misconceptions about HCV compared to influenza and retroviruses.

delivery_notes: All activities are designed for self-directed asynchronous online study within a virtual learning environment (VLE). Each activity includes clear materials and tasks suitable for individual work, with scaffolded reasoning and self-explanation prompts to enhance comprehension and retention.
